findings.description: Single Redis instance is a hidden single point of failure for all five microservices. All critical functions — session tokens, cart state, catalog cache, deduplication keys, and metrics — depend on one node. If Redis becomes unavailable, all services fail simultaneously.
findings.evidence: All five services will use a single shared Redis 7.x instance (`cache-primary.internal:6379`). Redis is deployed on a dedicated m5.2xlarge instance.

findings.description: The consequence 'Redis is always available so services can rely on cache reads succeeding' is an unverified assumption presented as a guarantee. No replication, failover, or availability SLA is specified. This assumption could propagate into service code that does not handle cache unavailability, causing hard failures instead of graceful degradation.
findings.evidence: Redis is always available so services can rely on cache reads succeeding

findings.description: Failure cost is borne by all five services and their users simultaneously. A single Redis hardware failure, OOM condition, or network partition causes complete loss of session auth, shopping carts, catalog browsing, notification dedup, and analytics at the same time.
findings.evidence: All five services share one instance. A single m5.2xlarge host failure takes down all dependent functions across the entire platform.

findings.description: No monitoring, alerting, or circuit-breaker mechanism for Redis is described. Failure would propagate silently until services begin returning errors to users, with no early detection.
findings.evidence: The ADR mentions 'one Redis instance to monitor' but specifies no monitoring implementation, alerting thresholds, or circuit-breaker/fallback strategy.

findings.description: No backup or recovery strategy is described for Redis data. Session tokens, cart state, and idempotency keys are stateful; losing them causes user-visible harm (forced logouts, lost carts, duplicate order processing) that cannot be recovered without backups.
findings.evidence: Mentions 'one Redis instance to back up' as a benefit but provides no backup frequency, retention, or restore procedure.

findings.description: Mixing volatile cache data (product catalog) with durable coordination data (idempotency keys, session tokens) in the same Redis instance creates a resource-contention coupling. A cache stampede or large catalog flush could evict idempotency keys, leading to duplicate order processing.
findings.evidence: Product catalog cache (potentially large, ephemeral) and idempotency keys (must be durable, small) share the same 32GB RAM pool with no described eviction policy segmentation or memory partitioning.

findings.description: Consul service discovery health checks deregister failed instances after 30 seconds, but there is no corresponding fallback or deregistration mechanism for the Redis dependency. Services can be 'healthy' from Consul's perspective while Redis is down, routing traffic to instances that will fail on every request.
findings.evidence: Failed instances are automatically removed from the service mesh — this applies to service instances, not to their shared dependencies like Redis.

findings.description: Failure is unbounded in blast radius. The single Redis instance constrains all five services. A partial failure (e.g. Redis slowing due to memory pressure) degrades the entire platform rather than one service.
findings.evidence: All five services will use a single shared Redis 7.x instance. 32GB RAM shared across all workloads.

clean.location: lines 25-27 (Consul health checks and service deregistration)
